issues.title: Non-Constant-Time Token Comparison Enables Timing Side-Channel Attack
issues.description: The gateway uses a strict JavaScript inequality operator (!==) to compare the client-provided token against the configured auth token in token mode. JavaScript string comparison is not constant-time and leaks information about how many leading characters match. The hooks endpoint has the same defect. Only password mode is protected via the timingSafeEqual-based safeEqual() helper.
issues.recommendation: Replace the strict inequality on line 175 of auth.ts with the existing safeEqual() helper: if (!safeEqual(connectAuth.token, auth.token)). Apply the same fix to the hooks token comparison in server-http.ts line 76: replace (token !== hooksConfig.token) with (!safeEqual(token, hooksConfig.token)). Both comparisons need Buffer.from() wrapping as already done for passwords.
issues.evidence: auth.ts line 175 uses !== directly. safeEqual() calls crypto.timingSafeEqual() at line 31-33 but is only invoked for password mode at line 189. The hooks check at server-http.ts line 76 also uses !==. Both are confirmed in the source; password mode is correctly guarded while token mode is not.

issues.title: Tailscale Identity Header Spoofing Bypasses Authentication
issues.description: The authorizeGatewayConnect function grants full authentication when three conditions are met: the source IP is loopback, the x-forwarded-for/x-forwarded-proto/x-forwarded-host headers are all present, and the tailscale-user-login header is non-empty. Any local process on the same host (including low-privileged ones) can satisfy all three conditions by sending a crafted HTTP request to localhost:18789, bypassing gateway token or password authentication entirely. The function accepts the headers at face value with no Tailscale HMAC validation.
issues.recommendation: Only accept Tailscale identity headers when the gateway is listening on a separate Tailscale-specific port, not on the shared loopback port. If using loopback, verify the HMAC signature Tailscale injects into requests, or bind the Tailscale-exposed service on a separate listener that never handles unauthenticated local connections.
issues.evidence: isTailscaleProxyRequest (auth.ts line 97-100) returns true for any loopback connection that includes all three forwarded headers, regardless of which process sent them. getTailscaleUser (line 74-86) reads the tailscale-user-login header without any cryptographic validation. authorizeGatewayConnect (line 142-161) grants auth method="tailscale" when both conditions pass, bypassing all token/password checks.

issues.title: Device Auth Payload Lacks Cryptographic Signature Allowing Role Escalation
issues.description: The buildDeviceAuthPayload function constructs an authentication payload by pipe-delimited string concatenation of version, deviceId, clientId, clientMode, role, scopes, timestamp, token, and optional nonce. There is no HMAC or digital signature applied to the payload. An attacker who observes a legitimate payload can construct a forged one with an elevated role (e.g., 'admin') and a matching token field. The v2 nonce only provides replay protection for non-loopback connections and does not protect payload integrity.
issues.recommendation: Sign the payload with HMAC-SHA256 using the gateway auth token as the signing key. Append the signature as an additional pipe-delimited field. Verify the signature server-side before accepting any device registration. This transforms the payload from a tamper-prone string into a verifiable token.
issues.evidence: device-auth.ts lines 13-31: the payload is purely a join() of input fields with no integrity protection. The token field is included as a field value but the entire payload is unsigned, meaning any attacker who knows the token can construct a new payload with arbitrary role/scopes. Verified in message-handler.ts lines 384-388 where buildDeviceAuthPayload is called with role from the connect request without server-side signature verification.

issues.title: Config Include Files Loaded Without Enforcing Strict Permissions (Audit-Only, Not Hard Block)
issues.description: The security audit system detects writable or world-readable config include files and reports them as critical findings, but the config loader continues to load and apply the include files regardless. An attacker who can modify any included config fragment (e.g., a CI-managed snippet with group-writable permissions) can change auth.mode, tools.elevated.allowFrom, or hooks.token values that take effect on the next hot-reload or gateway restart without any hard block.
issues.recommendation: Move the permission check into the config loader itself (src/config/includes.ts) so that loading an include file with group-writable or world-writable permissions results in a hard error that aborts the load. Treat permission failures as parse errors, not advisory findings.
issues.evidence: collectIncludeFilePermFindings (audit-extra.ts line 701) confirms the finding-only approach: it returns SecurityAuditFinding objects but does not throw or return an error that prevents loading. The config loader processes includes without any permission gate. An attacker modifying an include file would have their changes applied silently at the next reload.

issues.title: SSRF via URL Input - Cloud Metadata Endpoint (169.254.x.x) Not Blocked
issues.description: The fetchWithGuard function in input-files.ts implements SSRF protection via assertPublicHostname(), which resolves DNS and checks the resolved IP against a private address list. However, the PRIVATE_IPV4_PATTERNS list omits the 169.254.0.0/16 link-local range, which includes the AWS EC2 instance metadata service (169.254.169.254), Azure IMDS (169.254.169.254), and GCP metadata (169.254.169.254). An authenticated API user can trigger a fetch to http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ that bypasses the guard and returns cloud credentials.
issues.recommendation: Add /^169\.254\./ to PRIVATE_IPV4_PATTERNS to block the entire link-local range. Also add patterns for 100.64.0.0/10 (CGNAT/Tailscale) and any other special-purpose ranges per RFC 6890. Additionally, validate that the resolved IP at connection time matches the pre-checked IP to prevent DNS rebinding between the check and the connect.
issues.evidence: PRIVATE_IPV4_PATTERNS at input-files.ts lines 110-116 does not include 169.254.x.x. assertPublicHostname() at line 138 uses DNS lookup to resolve the hostname before checking, but the resolved address is checked only against this incomplete list. Fetching http://169.254.169.254/ directly (which resolves to 169.254.169.254) would pass isPrivateIpAddress() at line 119-126 and proceed to fetch.

issues.title: Unrestricted Session Key Header Allows Cross-Session Context Injection
issues.description: The resolveSessionKey function reads an arbitrary session key from the x-clawdbot-session-key HTTP header and uses it directly without any ownership validation. An authenticated API caller can set this header to any value including session keys belonging to other users' messaging channel sessions (e.g., telegram:123456789:dm:main), injecting messages into or reading conversation history from those sessions.
issues.recommendation: Validate that any explicitly provided session key is scoped to the authenticated user's identity. For API sessions, only allow keys that match the api:, openai:, or openresponses: namespace prefixes. Reject any key that matches known messaging channel session key patterns (e.g., telegram:, whatsapp:, discord:).
issues.evidence: resolveSessionKey at http-utils.ts line 58-59 returns the header value verbatim without any validation. This is called in openai-http.ts and openresponses-http.ts. The session key directly controls which conversation history and context is loaded for the agent run, confirmed by its use as the sessionKey parameter to agentCommand() in both HTTP handlers.

issues.title: No Rate Limiting on Hook and HTTP API Endpoints
issues.description: The gateway HTTP server (server-http.ts) dispatches hook and AI API requests without any per-token, per-IP, or per-session rate limiting. Each valid hook request triggers an async AI agent run that fires-and-forgets (returns 202 immediately). An attacker with a valid hook token can flood the endpoint, driving unlimited AI provider API costs and exhausting gateway resources.
issues.recommendation: Implement a per-token sliding-window rate limiter on hooks and OpenAI/OpenResponses endpoints. Log the requesting IP, timestamp, and token hash for every hook dispatch. Add a configurable maximum concurrent agent runs limit with queue-based backpressure.
issues.evidence: createHooksRequestHandler in server-http.ts processes each request immediately after token validation with no rate-limiting logic. dispatchAgentHook returns a runId synchronously after queuing the run, then returns 202 with no throttling applied. The function is called for every valid POST without any counter or time-based gate.

issues.title: Internal Error Messages Leaked Verbatim in HTTP 500 Responses
issues.description: Multiple HTTP handlers catch arbitrary exceptions and return String(err) directly in the JSON response body. Node.js Error objects often contain file system paths, module names, configuration values, and stack traces. This allows authenticated callers to extract internal deployment paths and service topology by triggering specific error conditions.
issues.recommendation: Replace String(err) in all catch blocks with a generic message (e.g., 'An internal error occurred'). Log the full error server-side with a correlation ID, and return only the correlation ID in the response so errors can be debugged without leaking internals. Apply this fix to openresponses-http.ts lines 572 and 854, openai-http.ts lines 241-243 and 364-376, and the top-level catch in server-http.ts line 261.
issues.evidence: openresponses-http.ts line 572: error: { code: 'api_error', message: String(err) } returned in a 500 JSON body. openai-http.ts line 242: sendJson(res, 500, { error: { message: String(err), type: 'api_error' } }). openai-http.ts line 372: delta: { content: `Error: ${String(err)}` } emitted in SSE stream. server-http.ts line 261: res.end(String(err)) at the top-level error handler.

issues.title: Silent Loopback-to-0.0.0.0 Fallback Exposes Gateway When Loopback Unavailable
issues.description: resolveGatewayBindHost in net.ts falls back to binding on 0.0.0.0 when the loopback mode is configured but 127.0.0.1 cannot be bound (canBindTo returns false). This fail-open behavior silently exposes the gateway to all network interfaces in containerized or unusual network environments where loopback is unavailable, potentially exposing an unauthenticated gateway to the container network.
issues.recommendation: Replace the fallback with a hard failure: when loopback mode is configured but 127.0.0.1 cannot be bound, throw an error and exit rather than silently binding to 0.0.0.0. Log a clear message directing the operator to configure bind=lan explicitly if they need all-interface binding.
issues.evidence: net.ts line 33-34: `if (await canBindTo('127.0.0.1')) return '127.0.0.1'; return '0.0.0.0';` The comment 'extreme fallback' acknowledges this is dangerous. A container environment where loopback is not available would silently expose the gateway on all interfaces, bypassing the security model of loopback-only binding.

issues.title: Hook Authentication Token Exposed in URL Query Parameter
issues.description: The extractHookToken function in hooks.ts supports passing the authentication token as a plain URL query parameter (?token=...). Query parameters are routinely logged by web servers, reverse proxies, CDNs, and browser history. This allows the hook token to leak into access logs and request history, even when the token is never stored explicitly.
issues.code_snippet:
const queryToken = url.searchParams.get("token");
if (queryToken) return queryToken.trim();
issues.recommendation: Remove support for query-parameter token authentication. Require the token to be provided only via the Authorization: Bearer header or the X-Clawdbot-Token header, which are not routinely logged. If backwards compatibility requires the query parameter, document it as deprecated and add a warning when it is used.
issues.evidence: extractHookToken in hooks.ts lines 56-57 reads the token from url.searchParams. This is confirmed by the test at hooks.test.ts line 49: url.searchParams.get('token') is tested as a valid auth path. Any reverse proxy, CDN, or server log capturing the full request URL will include the token in plaintext.

issues.title: Dynamic Module Import from User-Configured Path Enables Code Injection via Config
issues.description: The applyHookMappings function dynamically imports JavaScript modules from filesystem paths specified in the hooks.mappings[].transform.module configuration field. The path is resolved relative to the config directory (or a configurable transformsDir). An attacker who can modify the configuration file or any writable include file can specify a malicious module path that executes arbitrary Node.js code when a matching hook request is received.
issues.recommendation: Restrict loadable transform modules to files within a pre-defined and operator-confirmed transforms directory. Validate that the resolved module path starts with an allowlisted base directory. Consider requiring modules to be explicitly enumerated in the config rather than accepting arbitrary file paths. Log all dynamic module loads at startup with the full resolved path.
issues.evidence: loadTransform at hooks-mapping.ts line 280-288 calls import(url) where url is derived directly from transform.modulePath, which is set at line 162 from mapping.transform.module (an operator config field). resolveHookMappings at line 104 builds this path using resolvePath(transformsDir, mapping.transform.module) which at line 298-302 accepts absolute paths unconditionally and resolves relative ones against the config directory. An attacker-modifiable config or writable include file could point this at any .js file on the filesystem.

issues.title: Open Group Policy Combined with Elevated Tools Enables Arbitrary Code Execution
issues.description: When a messaging channel is configured with groupPolicy=open and tools.elevated is enabled, any member of that channel can send messages that cause the AI agent to execute elevated tools including bash execution (execTool), browser automation, and system.run commands on connected devices. The security audit identifies this but it is enforced only as a warning, not a hard block at gateway startup or runtime.
issues.recommendation: Enforce this as a hard runtime block at gateway startup, not just as an audit finding. When tools.elevated.enabled is true, refuse to start any channel with groupPolicy=open. Display a clear error directing the operator to either disable elevated tools or set groupPolicy=allowlist.
issues.evidence: collectExposureMatrixFindings at audit-extra.ts line 876 produces a finding with severity='critical' but this is only advisory. The gateway starts and operates normally with this configuration. The finding is only visible when the operator manually runs the security audit command.

issues.title: canvas.eval Enabled by Default on All Platforms Allows Remote Code Execution on Connected Devices
issues.description: The CANVAS_COMMANDS list in node-command-policy.ts includes canvas.eval and it is included in the default allowlist for iOS, Android, macOS, and 'unknown' platforms. canvas.eval executes arbitrary JavaScript or code on connected native apps. This command is enabled by default and requires explicit operator configuration of denyCommands to disable, creating a dangerous default-on attack surface.
issues.recommendation: Remove canvas.eval from CANVAS_COMMANDS and all PLATFORM_DEFAULTS entries. Require explicit opt-in via allowCommands configuration. Add a startup-time warning when canvas.eval is explicitly enabled. Consider requiring exec-approval for all canvas.eval invocations.
issues.evidence: node-command-policy.ts lines 4-13: canvas.eval is in CANVAS_COMMANDS. Lines 31-57: PLATFORM_DEFAULTS for ios, android, macos, and unknown all spread CANVAS_COMMANDS, making canvas.eval available by default. resolveNodeCommandAllowlist at line 76 uses PLATFORM_DEFAULTS as the base, requiring explicit denyCommands to remove canvas.eval.
